questions.text: I feel most at ease when I am the one setting expectations.
I feel a genuine sense of accomplishment when I navigate an unplanned situation successfully.
I sense what someone needs to hear in a given moment based on their nonverbal cues.
I sometimes feel like my nervous system has no middle gear — it's either idling or redlining.
I make decisions by weighing the long-term consequences carefully before acting.
I experience loneliness as a quiet awareness rather than a sharp pang.
I believe that self-sacrifice is not a burden but a calling.
I find that surprises — even pleasant ones — send a shock through my system.
I find that I do my best work when I am focused on my own advancement.
I routinely put my health second to the needs of those who depend on me.
I am the one who usually proposes the plan for a group activity.
I find that engaging with human pain through an analytical framework rather than an emotional one allows me to act with greater precision and less bias.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral reality that persists even when circumstances make responding difficult, costly, or inconvenient.
I hold the view that my moral commitment to addressing suffering is best expressed through reliable, sustained action rather than through emotional intensity.
I find myself reading between the lines in most conversations.
I tend not to notice when someone's smile seems forced.
I prepare talking points before important conversations.
I tend to find ways to collaborate within a shared lane.
